food_name: Opor Ayam (Javanese Chicken in White Coconut Milk Curry)
food_subgroup: Chicken in mild white coconut milk curry sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Opor Ayam. Closest parent is chicken dish in mild coconut milk curry sauce. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken in mild white coconut milk curry with candlenut and coriander (Javanese opor ayam)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CHG used as closest parent for chicken-based coconut milk curry dish; no exact Opor Ayam entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Opor Ayam or equivalent Javanese coconut milk chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Opor Ayam is approximately 250 g, comprising a portion of braised chicken pieces with generous white coconut milk gravy, as typically served at Hari Raya celebrations alongside ketupat.

nutrient_source.title: Opor Ayam — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Regional Food Composition Table for chicken in coconut milk curry, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Malaysian-style opor ayam preparation. Both are approved primary regional sources. Values represent the combined chicken-plus-gravy dish as consumed.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — chicken curry with coconut milk (analogous estimate); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Opor Ayam in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 (low) is estimated from the closest analogous entry: mild chicken coconut milk curry with minimal starch content. The very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and high fat from coconut milk substantially blunt glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (54 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.3 g; however, the curry-only GL is approximately 1.7 g per 100 g basis. Estimation is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Opor Ayam to one moderate serving (150–200 g) per meal and avoid adding extra salt or shrimp paste, as a full 250 g serving contributes approximately 800 mg sodium — one-third of the 2,400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and high fat from coconut milk slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Opor Ayam itself has minimal glycaemic impact (GI ~54, GL ~1.7 per 100 g) due to its very low carbohydrate content; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the total meal glycaemic load is driven primarily by the accompanying ketupat or rice rather than the curry itself, and portion control of the starch component is the key management strategy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (8.5 g/100 g); a 250 g serving delivers 21.25 g saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Opor Ayam as an occasional food only, as a single 250 g serving delivers 21.25 g saturated fat — exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g — due to the intentionally coconut-milk-rich preparation style of this Javanese dish; smaller portions of 100–120 g are advisable if consumed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (8.5 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Opor Ayam to one small serving per Hari Raya occasion, as this is among the highest-saturated-fat chicken dishes due to the intentionally coconut-milk-rich preparation style, and a 250 g serving exceeds the 20 g/day saturated fat limit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Opor Ayam is the quintessential Javanese Lebaran (Eid al-Fitr) dish — a mild, creamy white chicken curry served at virtually every Hari Raya celebration across Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore. Unlike the spicy red curries of Indian or Padang cuisine, Opor Ayam is deliberately mild and white-yellow in colour, achieved by using a generous quantity of full-fat coconut milk and deliberately omitting dried chilli. The spice paste (bumbu) features candlenuts (kemiri / buah keras), ground coriander, galangal, lemongrass, turmeric, shallots, and garlic. Chicken pieces are braised slowly in the coconut milk spice broth until tender and deeply flavoured. The resulting dish is rich, aromatic, and subtly sweet from the coconut milk. Nutritionally, Opor Ayam is high in fat (14.5 g/100 g) with a very high saturated fat fraction (8.5 g/100 g) derived from full-fat coconut milk — making it the richest in saturated fat among common chicken curries. Protein content is substantial at 14.2 g/100 g from the chicken. Carbohydrate content is very low at 3.2 g/100 g, giving the dish a low glycaemic index (~54) and minimal glycaemic load. Sodium is moderate at 320 mg/100 g. Clinically, the primary concern is cardiovascular and inflammatory risk from high saturated fat intake, particularly at festive serving sizes. It is traditionally eaten with ketupat (compressed palm-leaf rice cake) during Hari Raya Aidilfitri and is a defining dish of Javanese culinary identity.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Regional Food Composition Table cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD — both approved primary regional sources. No single exact Opor Ayam entry exists in either database; values represent the closest adapted match for chicken in mild coconut milk curry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Opor Ayam (chicken in white coconut milk curry) is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared dish is not reported in the consulted approved sources. All other per_100g fields are populated from approved source data. GI value of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest comparable entry (mild chicken coconut milk curry); no direct GI measurement for Opor Ayam exists in the database. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (54 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 4.3 g. The input record listed GI confidence as 0.5, consistent with analogous-estimate status. FoodEx2 code A0CHG is used as closest parent; no exact Opor Ayam FoodEx2 entry exists. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept for Opor Ayam has been published. SNOMED CT code is null as no applicable clinical food concept exists for this dish.
